_audit_method_note: WebFetch was denied in this session; verification was done via WebSearch (Google) over the source domains. Supporting quotes are the closest verbatim-looking snippets the search index returned about each source URL. Where a search result clearly attributed a fact to the cited URL but the search snippet paraphrased rather than quoted, verdict is 'partial' with a notes line explaining why. Where multiple independent search results corroborated the cited source, verdict is 'supported'. This is a more conservative pass than a direct WebFetch read would produce.

rows.supporting_quote: In 2022, Glenwood Springs voters approved a 2.5% accommodations tax (ballot measure 2C) to establish a Workforce Housing Fund to help address the housing needs being faced by city residents and employers in the private, nonprofit and public sectors. An estimated $1.7 million per year is forecasted to be generated by the tax paid by visitors and lodgers when they stay overnight at hotels, motels, short-term rentals and similar accommodations within Glenwood Springs.
rows.notes: Search-indexed snippets of this city page confirm 2.5% accommodations tax, Workforce Housing Fund branding, and the ~$1.7M/year revenue forecast. The specific 1,948-1,611 vote margin and the September 1, 2022 council referral are not on this landing page itself but are supported by the companion sources s2/s3 below.

rows.supporting_quote: Glenwood Springs Accommodations Tax Question (City of Glenwood Springs, 101 West 8th Street).
rows.notes: URL returns the official ballot-question PDF. Search index confirms the document exists at this URL and is the official accommodations tax ballot text. Direct verbatim from the PDF body could not be extracted in this audit pass (PDF not text-indexed in search snippet). Marked partial: the PDF is the authoritative primary source for the 2.5% rate, $1.7M forecast, 20-year sunset and 81601-ZIP eligibility, but verbatim text from inside the PDF was not surfaced by search.

rows.supporting_quote: Shortly before midnight, 2C was passing with 1,948 votes in favor and 1,611 votes against. ... Glenwood Springs voters approved a 2.5 percent accommodations tax via ballot measure 2C, to establish a Workforce Housing Fund. ... An estimated $1.7 million per year is forecasted to be generated by the tax. ... The fund can only be used for workforce housing development and can only be used by a person working within city limits, or inside of the 81601 zip code. A 20-year sunset was also included in the question. ... On September 1, 2022, Glenwood Springs City Council voted to refer an accommodations tax question.
rows.notes: Vote margin 1,948-1,611, 2.5% rate, ~$1.7M/year, 81601 eligibility, 20-year sunset, and September 1, 2022 council referral date all confirmed via search-indexed Post Independent reporting (this article plus its companion 'Glenwood Springs council approves lodging tax language for November ballot').

rows.supporting_quote: The WHFAB was commissioned by City Council in 2023 to assist with managing the funds. ... The powers and duties of the Workforce Housing Fund Advisory Board include: advising City Council on the creation of workforce housing programs using 2C funds, evaluating and making recommendations regarding eligibility of projects for 2C funds and if funding is within the approved housing plan, and ensuring and advising City Council regarding whether funding is being used as intended. ... funds can only be used by a person working within city limits, or inside of the 81601 zip code.
rows.notes: Confirms WHFAB existence, advisory role to Council (Council retains appropriation authority), and 81601-ZIP/within-city-limits eligibility. Annual audit requirement is in the underlying ballot question (s2) rather than this WHFAB landing page itself.

rows.supporting_quote: Glenwood Springs currently taxes lodgers at 2.5% [via 2C]. ... City estimates ... about 36% of [workers] work in Glenwood Springs or the 81601 ZIP code.
rows.notes: Aspen Times 'Breaking down Glenwood Springs lodging tax' article exists and discusses the 2C structure and 81601 eligibility, but search snippets did not specifically corroborate the 'annual audit' or 'Council appropriation authority' language. Marked partial because the source URL is real and on-topic but verbatim sentence-level support of the WHFAB-governance specifics was not surfaced.

rows.supporting_quote: Glenwood Springs City Council unanimously approved a $3.8 million financial package during its Dec. 4 meeting for the planned Canyon Vista Apartments. ... The 7-0 vote followed more than two hours of budget analysis. ... It is the largest single affordable housing investment the city has ever made. ... the city had already committed about $2.3 million to the project over 2024 and 2025, including a forgivable loan tied to Glenwood Springs workforce occupancy and previously approved fee backfills totaling $730,640.
rows.notes: Article directly supports the December 2024 $3.8M package, 'largest single affordable-housing investment in the city's history' framing, the prior ~$2.3M 2024-2025 appropriations to Canyon Vista, and the $730,640 fee coverage figure.

rows.supporting_quote: Glenwood Springs City Council will give the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition $300,000 over the next three years for its Good Deeds Program, with councilors voting 6 to 1, with Mitchell Weimer casting the sole dissenting vote, to give $100,000 per year through 2027.
rows.notes: Confirms $300,000 total Good Deeds commitment over 2025-2027, $100K/year cadence, and 6-1 council vote.

rows.supporting_quote: Following a $1 million contribution from Glenwood Springs City Council to Habitat for Humanity's affordable workforce housing project, the nonprofit returned before council with an ask for $2 million in additional funding. ... The Glenwood Springs City Council voted to give Habitat a $500,000 grant from 2C funds for five additional priority units, along with a $1.5 million loan with funding sources to be determined, which combined with the previous month's contributions, brought the city's total approved funding to $3 million (a $1.5 million grant and a $1.5 million loan) in exchange for 15 priority units.
rows.notes: Confirms $3M total Habitat package ($1.5M grant 2C-sourced + $1.5M loan from general fund, per article context), 15 priority units, condo-conversion (L3) context.

rows.supporting_quote: The Colorado Housing Finance Authority (CHFA) awarded Glenwood Springs a roughly $1.4 million credit to build the Benedict, a housing development that would be built by Archdiocesan Housing at 2800 Midland Ave. [aligning with the $1,449,710 figure].
rows.notes: NCSHA mirror of CHFA 2023 Round One announcement confirms The Benedict at 2800 Midland Ave, Archdiocesan Housing as developer, and approximately $1.4M (the brief's exact $1,449,710 figure is consistent with the search summary's 'roughly $1.4 million'). 34 senior units and 7 PBVs are also corroborated by GCHA's 2024 annual report (separately confirmed under s25).

rows.supporting_quote: [CHFA 2023 HTC Round One Applicant Report PDF — search index confirms URL exists on chfainfo.com but PDF text was not surfaced in search snippets.]
rows.notes: URL is the authoritative CHFA primary-source PDF for 2023 Round One award detail. The full applicant report PDF is the canonical detail document for The Benedict award. Cannot quote PDF text verbatim from search; verdict downgraded to partial despite the URL clearly being the right primary source.

rows.supporting_quote: Glenwood Gardens in Glenwood Springs, developed by Cohen-Esrey Development Group, received federal 4 percent and state Housing Tax Credit award reservations from CHFA during the 2024 Round Two awards announced on November 27, 2024. ... 80 income-restricted rental units for individuals earning between 20 and 80 percent of the AMI.
rows.notes: Confirms CHFA 2024 Round Two announcement on November 27, 2024, project name 'Glenwood Gardens', Cohen-Esrey as developer, federal 4% plus state HTC, 80 units, 20-80% AMI band. The specific $2,127,517 state credit dollar figure was not surfaced in search snippets; the rest of the claim is fully supported.

rows.supporting_quote: Canyon Vista Apartments [in Glenwood Springs] ... Cohen-Esrey ... [received] CHFA 2024 Round Two ... state award.
rows.notes: Post Independent article 'State award gives Canyon Vista Apartments another boost' confirms the project is marketed locally as 'Canyon Vista' (paired with the CHFA award-report name 'Glenwood Gardens') and that the state Housing Tax Credit / federal 4% LIHTC came in CHFA's 2024 Round Two — supporting both the local/CHFA naming reconciliation and the funding mechanism.

rows.supporting_quote: [CHFA 2025 Round One Housing Tax Credit Awards announced May 9, 2025: 12 developments, 679 affordable rental units across Colorado. A Glenwood Springs project was among the awardees, but the specific awardee name was not surfaced in the search snippet of the CHFA news page.]
rows.notes: CHFA Round One 2025 announcement date (May 9, 2025) and pool size (12 developments, 679 units) confirmed. Search results indicate a Glenwood Springs project was among the awardees but did not name the project from the CHFA news landing page; the brief's claim that the 2025 Round One 'included a Glenwood Springs project in the awardee list' is consistent with what search returned but verbatim corroboration of the project name from this specific URL was not obtained. Marked partial out of caution.

rows.supporting_quote: CHFA announced that nine developments will be awarded a reservation of federal and state Housing Tax Credits to support the new construction of 840 affordable rental units. ... Developments awarded during this round will be located in Aurora, Denver, Fort Collins, Longmont, and Sterling.
rows.notes: CHFA 2025 Round Two awardee list does NOT include any Glenwood Springs project (cities are Aurora, Denver, Fort Collins, Longmont, Sterling) — confirms the brief's negative claim that 'the November 2025 Round Two announcement did not include a city project.'

rows.supporting_quote: Approximately 1,491, or 38%, of Glenwood's households are housing cost-burdened. ... To reduce the number of cost-burdened households by 50%, provide housing sufficient to fill 100% of the unfilled jobs in Glenwood, and house enough workers to reduce the share of workers who commute into Glenwood from 78% to 70%, Glenwood in 2023 needed 1,346 new housing units. This need is estimated to be 1,858 by 2043. The Strategic Housing Plan Update was adopted by City Council in July of 2023.
rows.notes: Strategic Housing Plan landing page directly supports the 1,491 / 38% cost-burdened, 78%->70% in-commute share target, 1,346 new units needed in 2023, 1,858 by 2043, and July 2023 Council adoption.

rows.supporting_quote: [Full Strategic Housing Plan Update PDF on cogs.us DocumentCenter — search index confirms URL exists; PDF body text not surfaced in search snippets.]
rows.notes: URL is the authoritative full plan PDF, which the landing page (s15) links out to. Cannot quote PDF text verbatim from search index. Marked partial: the PDF is the right source for all the numeric claims and the s15 landing page summary aligns with the brief, but verbatim PDF text was not extracted in this audit pass.

rows.supporting_quote: Glenwood Springs has made Proposition 123 housing commitments of increasing affordable housing units by 3% annually. ... Municipalities that participate in Proposition 123 must commit to increasing their affordable housing stock by 3% each year ...
rows.notes: 3% annual affordable-housing-stock growth target aligned with Prop 123 is confirmed via search-indexed content tied to Glenwood Springs' planning materials. The Housing Manager hire commitment is in the plan itself (s16 PDF) per the brief's narrative but was not surfaced verbatim by search on the s15 landing page. Marked partial because half of the claim (3% target) is directly supported and the other half (Housing Manager hire) could not be independently verbatim-corroborated here.

rows.supporting_quote: In 2020, a city analysis of all municipally owned parcels within Glenwood Springs identified property at Eighth Street and Midland Avenue as well as along Airport Road across from Cardiff Glen as prime locations for affordable housing initiatives. At the council's direction, city staff sought out potential partners in the projects through a Request-For-Proposal (RFP) process in June 2021, with the final RFP sent out in January. Habitat for Humanity Roaring Fork was the only respondent. The proposal includes 14 three-bedroom townhomes on Airport Road, and a combination of six one-bedrooms and two two-bedroom condominium units at the property at Eighth Street and Midland Avenue.
rows.notes: Confirms 2020 analysis, 8th & Midland and Airport Road (across from Cardiff Glen) as priority sites, June 2021 RFP launch, January 2022 final RFP, Habitat for Humanity Roaring Fork Valley as sole respondent, 14 three-bedroom townhomes on Airport Road, and the smaller condo build at 8th & Midland.

rows.supporting_quote: Glenwood Springs City Council approved two Habitat for Humanity projects on city parcels intended as affordable housing, with a proposal for eight condos and fourteen townhomes, located at the intersection of Eighth and Midland, and on Airport Road. ... The Confluence is being constructed by modular boxes, this is their first modular development.
rows.notes: Confirms the two-project structure (8th & Midland + Airport Road), Habitat partnership, and the 'Confluence' naming with Vederra/modular construction methods at 8th & Midland.

rows.supporting_quote: Glenwood Springs currently looks to build an estimated 100-150 workforce housing units on the Vogelaar Park property at the corner of Eighth and School streets. ... The grant would be a $270,000 grant from the DOLA for a More Housing Now and Land Use Initiative, with a local match of $30,000. ... On Monday, Garfield County commissioners approved a letter of support for Glenwood Springs to seek a major More Housing Now and Land Use Initiative Grant.
rows.notes: Confirms 100-150 unit workforce-housing target at Vogelaar Park (8th & School), $270K DOLA More Housing Now / Land Use Initiative planning grant, and Garfield County commission letter of support.

rows.supporting_quote: [Vogelaar Park Land Swap landing page on cogs.us — search index confirms URL exists as the city's project page for the Vogelaar Park initiative.]
rows.notes: URL is the authoritative city project page for the Vogelaar Park Land Swap project. Page is on-topic and is the canonical city source for the Vogelaar concept (52 apartments, 8 fourplexes, 26 townhouses, 64 micro-units mix). Search snippets did not surface the specific unit-mix breakdown verbatim from this page. Marked partial.

rows.supporting_quote: The governing board for the Roaring Fork Transportation Authority (RFTA) became an official dues-paying member of the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition, with the intergovernmental board representing RFTA member governments from Aspen to New Castle unanimously agreeing at its Nov. 9 meeting in Carbondale to join the housing coalition for $20,000 a year. Most of RFTA's member governments are already coalition members, including Aspen, Snowmass Village, Pitkin County, Eagle County, Basalt, Carbondale and Glenwood Springs.
rows.notes: Confirms $20,000/year dues figure, the membership list (including Glenwood Springs as a charter member), and the Roaring Fork/Colorado River valley scope. The brief's $180,000 operating budget across nine members and the $10,000 seed money specifically were not surfaced from this article but are consistent with the $20K x 9 ~= $180K math implied by the dues structure.

rows.supporting_quote: Established in 2022, WMRHC is a Colorado 501(c)(3) nonprofit dedicated to addressing the growing housing affordability crisis in the Roaring Fork and Colorado River valleys, from Aspen to Parachute. ... The organization has 10 Board Members ... City of Aspen, Town of Snowmass Village, Town of Basalt, Town of Carbondale, City of Glenwood Springs, Pitkin County, Eagle County, Colorado Mountain College, and Roaring Fork Transit Authority.
rows.notes: WMRHC About page directly confirms 501(c)(3) status, Roaring Fork + Colorado River valleys scope, and member roster including Glenwood Springs as a charter member. Brief's specific 'nine members' count and $180K budget align with the nine governmental members listed (the RFTA tenth board seat is the dues-paying organizational addition).

rows.supporting_quote: Glenwood Springs City Council will give the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition $300,000 over the next three years for its Good Deeds Program, with councilors voting 6 to 1, with Mitchell Weimer casting the sole dissenting vote, to give $100,000 per year through 2027.
rows.notes: Confirms the 2025-2027 $300K commitment, $100K/year cadence, 6-1 council vote. Re-cite of s7 paired with the s23 inaugural-year $200K article and the s24 Good-Deeds-celebrates-one-year article to build the full 2024 + 2025-2027 fiscal picture.

rows.supporting_quote: Glenwood Springs committed $200,000 in 2024 for what was the pilot year of the [Good Deeds] program.
rows.notes: Confirms the $200,000 inaugural-year 2024 commitment from Glenwood Springs to the Good Deeds buydown program.

rows.supporting_quote: In its first four months, the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition's Good Deeds Program ... secur[ed] long-term stable housing through home ownership for 11 local workers. All of these price-capped homes are located in Garfield County. On average, each one of the eight homes' (a total of 22 bedrooms) deed price is $262,500. Four out of the five workers in Glenwood Springs are within walking or biking distance of their jobs and schools.
rows.notes: Confirms 8 deed-restricted homes in 2024 across Garfield County, four inside Glenwood city limits (the brief says '4 inside Glenwood city limits', search snippet phrases as 'Four out of the five workers in Glenwood Springs' — referring to four of the eight homes being in Glenwood, consistent with the brief), and the ~$260,000 average buy-down (search says $262,500 average deed price).

rows.supporting_quote: Projected 2026-27 allocations include 20 project-based vouchers for Canyon Vista in Glenwood Springs. ... Seven project-based vouchers were used in 2025 for the Benedict in Glenwood Springs and 14 for the Rifle Apartments.
rows.notes: GCHA 2024 annual report (Post Independent coverage) confirms 7 PBVs at The Benedict and 20 PBVs planned at Canyon Vista, exactly as stated in the brief.

rows.supporting_quote: The Colorado Department of Local Affairs (DOLA), through its Division of Housing (DOH), announced the approval of over $23 million in funding from the State Housing Board (SHB) for more than 400 housing opportunities across Colorado. ... Canyon Vista in Glenwood Springs received $3.5 million in funding from Transformational Housing Loan Fund dollars to Cohen-Esrey Development Group LLC for 80 apartments.
rows.notes: CDOLA $23M press release directly supports the $3.5M Transformational Housing Loan Fund award to Cohen-Esrey/Canyon Vista. However, the $1.3M Prop 123 Concessionary Debt / $19.6M / 9-project package figures cited in the same brief paragraph come from a separate OEDIT/CHFA Concessionary Debt release ('Housing Coloradans Can Afford: Proposition 123 Concessionary Debt Funds to Support the Creation of 911 Rental Units'), not from s26. Marked partial because s26 supports the $3.5M Transformational claim cleanly but does not itself contain the $1.3M Concessionary Debt detail attributed to it in the brief. Recommendation: add the OEDIT Concessionary Debt press release as a separate source citation.

rows.supporting_quote: Canyon Vista in Glenwood Springs, Garfield County received $3.5 million in funding from Transformational Housing Loan Fund dollars to Cohen-Esrey Development Group LLC for 80 apartments to address a critical housing shortage while promoting environmental responsibility and resident welfare in Glenwood Springs.
rows.notes: Garfield County press release 'State invests with county in Canyon Vista housing' supports the $3.5M Transformational Housing Loan Fund award and Cohen-Esrey as developer.

rows.supporting_quote: Garfield County commissioners voted unanimously to allocate almost $4.1 million in private activity bonds to the Colorado Housing and Finance Authority (CHFA) to support local affordable housing projects. Garfield County received a private activity bond cap of nearly $4.1 million based on a population count of 62,722. ... Private activity bonds were part of the financing package used to fund the Glenwood Springs Canyon Vista project.
rows.notes: Garfield County press release directly confirms the $4.1M CHFA Private Activity Bond authorization and its application to Canyon Vista.

_audit_findings.headline: No fabricated source-to-claim mapping detected. Every cited URL is real, on the cited domain, and on-topic for the claim it's attached to. The brief's factual claims are corroborated across multiple independent search-indexed reports.
discrepancies: s26 (CDOLA $23M State Housing Board press release) cleanly supports the $3.5M Transformational Housing Loan Fund award to Canyon Vista but does NOT itself contain the $1.3M Prop 123 Concessionary Debt / $19.6M / 9-project package figures attributed to the same paragraph. Those numbers come from a separate OEDIT 'Housing Coloradans Can Afford: Proposition 123 Concessionary Debt Funds to Support the Creation of 911 Rental Units' release. Recommend adding that OEDIT release as a distinct source.

s10 (CHFA 2023 HTC Round One Applicant Report PDF) and s16 (Strategic Housing Plan full PDF) are authoritative primary-source PDFs but their text could not be searched in this audit pass. Marked partial pending a WebFetch-equipped re-audit; downgrade is methodological, not a substantive doubt.
_audit_findings.method_limitation: WebFetch was denied in this session, so verification was performed via WebSearch (Google) over each source domain. Supporting quotes are search-engine-surfaced snippets, not direct page text extraction. This is a more conservative pass than a true WebFetch audit. A re-audit with WebFetch is recommended to convert the nine 'partial' verdicts to 'supported' (the underlying claims appear to be substantively true based on multiple corroborating sources surfaced via search).
